meta.description: Periodic table elements Francium through Oganesson — Period 7 complete. All actinides and synthetic superheavy elements. The periodic table is now complete.

elements.name: Francium
elements.origin_of_name: France — discovered by a French scientist
elements.mineral_role: No mineral role — trace in uranium ore from actinium decay; half-life 22 minutes
elements.notes: Most unstable naturally occurring element. Half-life of only 22 minutes. Discovered by Marguerite Perey — the last natural element discovered. The element that barely holds itself together.

elements.name: Radium
elements.origin_of_name: Latin: radius (ray)
elements.mineral_role: Trace in pitchblende (uraninite) and carnotite ores
elements.notes: Discovered by Marie Curie — her second radioactive element. Glows faint blue in the dark from Cherenkov radiation. Used in early luminous watch dials until its toxicity was understood.

elements.name: Actinium
elements.origin_of_name: Greek: aktinos (ray, beam)
elements.mineral_role: Trace in uranium ores as decay product
elements.notes: Namesake of the actinide series. Glows blue in the dark from its intense radioactivity. The ray-bearer — gateway of the f-block actinides.

elements.name: Thorium
elements.origin_of_name: Norse: Thor (god of thunder)
elements.notes: Named after Thor. Thorium-232 is a fertile nuclear material — can be bred into fissile U-233. Three times more abundant than uranium. Monazite beaches carry thorium. Thunder made element.

elements.name: Protactinium
elements.origin_of_name: Greek: protos (first) + actinium (decays to actinium)
elements.mineral_role: Trace in uranium ores — extremely rare naturally
elements.notes: Proto-actinium — it decays into actinium. One of the rarest naturally occurring elements. Highly radioactive and toxic. The precursor element.

elements.name: Uranium
elements.origin_of_name: Planet Uranus, discovered 1781
elements.notes: Autunite and Torbernite are stunningly beautiful fluorescent minerals — vivid green under UV light. Uranium-235 is the fissile isotope that powers nuclear reactors and weapons. Named for Uranus, discovered just 8 years prior.

elements.name: Neptunium
elements.origin_of_name: Planet Neptune — beyond Uranus, as Np is beyond U
elements.mineral_role: Trace in uranium ores from neutron capture; first transuranic element
elements.notes: First transuranic element — beyond uranium, beyond the natural. Named for Neptune — the next planet out. The first step past the edge of natural matter.

elements.name: Plutonium
elements.origin_of_name: Planet Pluto — following Neptune in the solar system naming pattern
elements.mineral_role: Trace in uranium ores; found in small natural quantities in Oklo natural reactor (Gabon)
elements.notes: Six allotropic forms — more crystal phases than any other element. Fissile Pu-239 powers nuclear weapons and reactors. Named for Pluto. The most complex element in existence.

elements.name: Americium
elements.origin_of_name: The Americas
elements.mineral_role: No natural mineral occurrence — purely synthetic
elements.notes: Am-241 is in every household smoke detector — its alpha particles ionize air to detect smoke. The most widely distributed transuranic element in the world.

elements.name: Curium
elements.discoverer: Glenn T. Seaborg, Ralph A. James, Albert Ghiorso
elements.origin_of_name: Marie and Pierre Curie
elements.mineral_role: No natural mineral occurrence — purely synthetic
elements.notes: Named in honor of Marie and Pierre Curie. Alpha particle power source in space probes. Curium-244 RTGs powered early deep space missions. Radioactive legacy of the greatest scientist.

elements.name: Berkelium
elements.discoverer: Glenn T. Seaborg, Stanley G. Thompson, Albert Ghiorso
elements.origin_of_name: Berkeley, California — University of California
elements.mineral_role: No natural mineral occurrence — purely synthetic
elements.notes: Named for Berkeley, California. Only microgram quantities ever produced. Required to synthesize tennessine (Z=117). A link in the superheavy synthesis chain.

elements.name: Californium
elements.origin_of_name: California and University of California
elements.mineral_role: No natural mineral occurrence — purely synthetic
elements.notes: Most powerful neutron emitter known. Used to start up nuclear reactors and treat certain cancers. One microgram emits 170 million neutrons per minute. A neutron sun in elemental form.

elements.name: Einsteinium
elements.origin_of_name: Albert Einstein
elements.mineral_role: No natural mineral occurrence — first found in fallout of 1952 Ivy Mike hydrogen bomb test
elements.notes: Named for Einstein. Discovered in the fallout of the first hydrogen bomb — Ivy Mike, 1952. Born from a thermonuclear explosion. The element that came from the bomb.

elements.name: Fermium
elements.origin_of_name: Enrico Fermi — father of nuclear reactor
elements.mineral_role: No natural occurrence — also found in Ivy Mike thermonuclear fallout
elements.notes: Named for Enrico Fermi. Also discovered in Ivy Mike hydrogen bomb fallout. The 100th element — a centennial of atomic matter.

elements.name: Mendelevium
elements.origin_of_name: Dmitri Mendeleev — creator of the periodic table
elements.notes: Named for Mendeleev — the man who created the table this element lives in. A beautiful recursion: the table honoring the one who built the table.

elements.name: Nobelium
elements.origin_of_name: Alfred Nobel — inventor of dynamite, founder of Nobel Prize
elements.notes: Named for Alfred Nobel. Last of the full actinide f-block sequence. The element that closes the lanthanide-analogue series.

elements.name: Lawrencium
elements.origin_of_name: Ernest Lawrence — inventor of the cyclotron
elements.notes: Named for Ernest Lawrence, inventor of the cyclotron — the machine that made most of these elements possible. Last of the actinides. The door to the superheavies.

elements.name: Rutherfordium
elements.discoverer: Albert Ghiorso et al. (LBNL) / Flerov et al. (JINR)
elements.origin_of_name: Ernest Rutherford — discovered the atomic nucleus
elements.mineral_role: No natural occurrence — purely synthetic, half-life ~1.3 hours
elements.notes: First transactinide element. Named for Rutherford — the man who discovered the nucleus. First element where superheavy chemistry begins.

elements.name: Dubnium
elements.origin_of_name: Dubna, Russia — Joint Institute for Nuclear Research
elements.notes: Named for Dubna, Russia — site of JINR where it was synthesized. Half-life ~28 hours. Cold War competition between American and Soviet labs shaped early superheavy element naming.

elements.name: Seaborgium
elements.origin_of_name: Glenn T. Seaborg — discoverer of 10 elements, received Nobel Prize
elements.notes: Named for Glenn Seaborg — the only person to have an element named for them while still alive. He discovered or co-discovered 10 elements. The element named for the element-maker.

elements.name: Bohrium
elements.origin_of_name: Niels Bohr — father of quantum atomic model
elements.notes: Named for Niels Bohr — whose model of the atom made quantum mechanics possible. The element named for the man who first described how electrons orbit.

elements.name: Hassium
elements.origin_of_name: Latin: Hassia (Hesse, Germany) — state where GSI is located
elements.notes: Named for Hesse, Germany. Predicted to be a solid at room temperature and behave like osmium chemically. The densest element predicted — estimated ~40.7 g/cm³.

elements.name: Meitnerium
elements.origin_of_name: Lise Meitner — co-discoverer of nuclear fission
elements.notes: Named for Lise Meitner — who explained nuclear fission theoretically but was denied the Nobel Prize given to her collaborator. An element that honors a wronged genius.

elements.name: Darmstadtium
elements.origin_of_name: Darmstadt, Germany — location of GSI
elements.notes: Named for Darmstadt — the city of its birth. Exists for microseconds. Known only from a few atoms ever synthesized.

elements.name: Roentgenium
elements.origin_of_name: Wilhelm Röntgen — discoverer of X-rays
elements.notes: Named for Röntgen — discoverer of X-rays in 1895. Gold group analog. The element that honors the man who let us see through matter.

elements.name: Copernicium
elements.origin_of_name: Nicolaus Copernicus — heliocentric model of the solar system
elements.notes: Named for Copernicus. May be a gas at room temperature due to strong relativistic effects — a metal that might behave like a noble gas. The element that breaks its own category.

elements.name: Nihonium
elements.origin_of_name: Japanese: Nihon (Japan) — first element discovered in Asia
elements.notes: First element discovered in Asia. Named for Japan — Nihon. Confirmed in 2016 after years of synthesis attempts at RIKEN. A point of national pride encoded into the periodic table.

elements.name: Flerovium
elements.origin_of_name: Flerov Laboratory of Nuclear Reactions, Dubna
elements.notes: On the predicted ‘island of stability’ — where superheavy nuclei may have longer half-lives. May behave more like a noble gas than a metal due to extreme relativistic effects.

elements.name: Moscovium
elements.origin_of_name: Moscow Oblast, Russia — location of JINR
elements.notes: Named for Moscow Oblast. Synthesized by colliding calcium-48 ions with americium-243 targets. Exists for milliseconds. An element born and gone in the blink of a thought.

elements.name: Livermorium
elements.origin_of_name: Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, California
elements.notes: Named for Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory. A joint Russian-American discovery — Cold War rivals collaborating to push matter to its edge.

elements.name: Tennessine
elements.origin_of_name: Tennessee — home of Oak Ridge National Laboratory and Vanderbilt University
elements.mineral_role: No natural occurrence — required berkelium-249 target, made in Oak Ridge reactor
elements.notes: Required specially produced Bk-249 from Oak Ridge to synthesize. Named for Tennessee. One of the most recently confirmed elements (2016). The element that required another element to exist.

elements.name: Oganesson
elements.origin_of_name: Yuri Oganessian — pioneer of superheavy element research
elements.mineral_role: No natural occurrence — purely synthetic. Only 5 atoms ever confirmed.
elements.notes: The last element. Z=118. Noble gas by position but predicted to be solid due to relativistic effects. Only 5 atoms ever confirmed to exist. Named for Yuri Oganessian while still living. The edge of the known periodic table. Beyond here, the database waits for what comes next.
